title: Logan Lay’s CV
text:
Jan 2026 - Apr 2026 Gas Station Shopper Segmentation — DS 460, Big Data Programming
Engineered complex features for a PySpark clustering model using academic research data from Dewey, including average in-store spend per visit and shoppers’ most frequently purchased product categories. Applied segmentation logic to classify shopper behavior across categories such as packaged drinks, tobacco, and fuel, enabling targeted marketing group analysis. Visualized clustering results and feature distributions with Plotly to communicate insights across shopper segments.
Sep 2025 - Dec 2025 Trail and Hiker SQL Database — ITM 220, SQL
Developed a relational database in MySQL Workbench, designing interconnected tables with proper data types and relationships. Wrote advanced queries using window functions, CASE statements, and common table expressions to extract and analyze targeted information. Applied JOIN operations and built-in functions to efficiently retrieve and manipulate data across multiple tables.

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
section: Data Wrangling for Three Most Common Reported UFO Shapes by State
text:
Data Wrangling for Three Most Common Reported UFO Shapes by State


Show the code
shape_dist_state <- dat %>% 
  filter(country == "us",
    !state %in% c("pr", "vi", "gu", "as", "mp", "dc", "hi", "ak")
    ) %>% 
  drop_na(shape) %>% 
  group_by(shape, state) %>% 
  summarize(count = n()) %>% 
  group_by(state) %>% 
  slice_max(count, n = 3, with_ties = FALSE) %>% 
  mutate(
    state = str_to_upper(state),
    shape = str_to_title(shape)
    )

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
text:
Insights
Seeing a “light” is the most common description of the sightings, while we have other repeated shapes, such as triangles and circles. It is interesting to see that the top reported shape has been consistent across both the years they were reported and the states they were reported in. For something that usually seems to be a misidentification, it has pretty decent consistency on the reported type of shape that these “extraterrestrials” fly.
Something I would like to further research with this data is how the reports evolve over time. Were the first years very similar? Were they isolated to one area of the US? At what times were these sightings reported to have been seen? Perhaps everyone claims they saw a light simply because that is what the first few people reported, and now it has become the norm.

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
section: Data Wrangling for UFO Sighting Report Map
text:
Data Wrangling for UFO Sighting Report Map


Show the code
# Getting county population data from US Census API
county_pop <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  variables = "B01003_001", # total population
  year = 2022, # match the county boundary year pull
  survey = "acs5"
) %>% 
  select(GEOID, estimate)


# Getting US county data
counties_sf <- counties(cb = TRUE, resolution = "20m", year = 2022)


# Filtering to be the main 48 states of spatial data
counties_sf <- counties_sf %>%
  filter(!STATEFP %in% c("02","15","72","60","66","69","78"))


# Getting county polygons to be in the right format
counties_sf <- st_transform(counties_sf, 4326)


# Filtering the UFO data to only be the main 48 states in the US
ufo_us <- dat %>% 
  filter(country == "us",
    !state %in% c("pr", "vi", "gu", "as", "mp", "dc", "hi", "ak")
    )


# Preparing UFO data for county aggregation; only keeping the main 48 states
ufo_counties <- dat %>% 
  filter(country == "us",
    !state %in% c("pr", "vi", "gu", "as", "mp", "dc", "hi", "ak")
    ) %>% 
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude","latitude"), crs = 4326) %>% 
  st_join(y=counties_sf, join=st_within) %>% 
  group_by(GEOID, NAME) %>% 
  summarize(ufo_sighting_count = n()) %>% 
  select(GEOID, ufo_sighting_count) %>% 
  st_drop_geometry()


# Joining UFO & Population County Data
counties_sf <- counties_sf %>% 
  left_join(y=ufo_counties, by="GEOID") %>% 
  mutate(ufo_sighting_count = coalesce(ufo_sighting_count, 0)) %>% 
  left_join(y=county_pop, by="GEOID")


# Calculating the Sighting Ratio
counties_sf <- counties_sf %>% 
  mutate(sighting_ratio = round(ufo_sighting_count / estimate * 1e5, 2))


# Creating a floor for the sighting/ratio so that smaller populations don't look ginormous
counties_sf <- counties_sf %>% 
  mutate(rate_display = ifelse(estimate < 5000, NA, sighting_ratio))


# Color Pallets
point_pal <- colorNumeric("Reds", domain = ufo_us$`duration (seconds)`)

county_pal <- colorBin("Reds", domain = counties_sf$rate_display, bins = c(0,1,5,10,25,50,100,250,Inf), na.color = "#f0f0f0")

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
text:
Insights
When I first got a hold of this data, I was really hoping to find that all the UFO sightings would be centralized around one location, and at first it seemed that seeing UFOs is a numbers game. When I looked at the data I came to the conclusion that the more people there are in an area, the more UFO sightings would be, which makes sense on its own. Something interesting happened with my ratio metric though.
If you take the time to look into the metrics that I gave on the map, you’ll notice that the more dense areas of the United States have the highest raw number of sightings, but the lowest rates per 100,000 people. The rural, low-population counties are where a given person is most likely to report something.
Additionally, I would love to input more county-level layers to test whether sighting rates correlate with other regional characteristics such as substance use prevalence, educational attainment, or median income. This is something I would have to be more careful with because any relationship would be correlational and subject to the ecological fallacy: county-level averages cannot establish anything about the individuals who filed reports.

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
text:
Dataset
Kaggle UFO Sightings

title: Final Project UFO Sightings
text:
AI Assistance Disclosure
Portions of this project were developed with assistance from Claude (Anthropic), accessed July 2026. The AI’s contributions were advisory and instructional rather than generative of the core analysis. Specifically, Claude helped with:

Conceptual guidance: explaining per-capita normalization and why raw sighting counts largely track population, the reporting-bias limitations of the worldwide data (motivating the decision to restrict analysis to the U.S.), and the small-denominator problem in low-population counties.
Methodological decisions: recommending county-level aggregation, a spatial join to assign points to counties, sourcing county boundaries (tigris) and population (tidycensus/ACS 5-year), and applying a population floor to prevent noisy rates from dominating the choropleth.
Technical explanation and debugging: clarifying R/sf functions (st_as_sf, st_join, coalesce, leaflet’s colorBin/addLegend), diagnosing errors (CRS mismatch, sf geometry persisting through joins, a re-run column collision), and explaining coordinate-system and per-100k scaling choices.

All analytical decisions, code implementation, data filtering, and interpretation were performed by the author. The AI did not select the dataset, run the analysis, or produce the final maps. Citation: Anthropic. (2026). Claude [large language model]. https://claude.ai
